speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 22nd, 1996. My name is Shauna Sherker, interviewing Rose Dora Presser in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia. Can you tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rose Dora Presser.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-R-E-S-S-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- what is your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ameisen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-M-E-S- I forgot the word. N-- I should-- Ameisen.

speaker: interviewer
text: A-M-E-I-S-E-N, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, Ameisen.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born, Rose?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1919, 21st of April.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old does that make you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, god. 75.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the name of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helena and Moishe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have four brothers and sister, one sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the family very close?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe a bit of your family life for me growing up in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. OK, It was very beautiful life. My mother was-- I have a very good parents. And my father was a very had working man. My mother was, too. Yes, the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] brothers, they all go to school. One went to France to study. One was studying in Poland. And one was working with my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of business did your family have?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] fabric. [NON-ENGLISH] was the name. Yes. My sister, [pauses] she learned cosmetics. Later, in '72, she went to Israel. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And my youngest brother was working in [NON-ENGLISH], in the technical business, you know? That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school, yes. Technical school, I finish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you studying?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I supposed to learn to-- to sew, you know? But later, I learned cosmetics in my auntie's place. And that's it. Then the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you live close to your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very close. I was every day over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the name of your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only know grandmother, my grandmother. But my grandfather died along time ago. Amelia, Amelia was her name. She was very sick. I was every day over there, my father, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this your father's mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father's mother, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do at-- at her house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was playing. I was little, maybe six, seven. She had the nurse. And she was very good. And she was in bed for eight years. She was paralyzed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- what street?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Szlak. The name was Szlak 13, Szlak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a Jewish neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the street to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: The street was-- very nice street in the corner. We have our factory. And it was a big building, maybe 100 tenants, a lot of kids. We play all day. And all the time, we-- we ran. It was beautiful street, very nice, full of life. It was little shul even in this-- inside. There was a few Jewish people living, yes. But not many around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family very religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was very religious, but my father was not. Father's side was not religion. But my mother was very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would your mother express her religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was keep kosher. First of all, she didn't eat in nobody-- only cup of tea she can drink when she goes somewhere. And she open-- the [NON-ENGLISH] with her own knife. That's it, nothing. Because we have the family who is not religious, when we have been invited, we have been eating. But she never eat nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she go to shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: She go to shul, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she insist that you go to shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We ran-- we like kids, we always come with her. Because she died, I was 12 when she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did she die?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did she die?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '31 I think, '31.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the cause of her death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father, when did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years later he died from heart attack.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your life change after your parents passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living with my brothers. I was working my auntie's place. She was a skin specialist. And she has an institute, cosmetic institute, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of your auntie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freida Ameisen. Yes. I have 10-- six uncles and four aunties. 10, very big family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they help take care of you when your parents were gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Very much, yes. They always knew that I wear warm panties. What panties you wearing today? She was fantastic person.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What type of interests did you have growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was playing tennis. I was skiing. I was swimming. Ice skating, not very well, but I skate. And that's it. I-- I went dancing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a dancing partner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I go very very often for dancing. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a--

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my husband on dancing, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, yes. What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband? Berek. Bernard Berek.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met him [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] '64 or '65-- no, what I am talking? '36? No. Yes. '36, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: '37, that's met him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did your-- were you in love right away? What was your feelings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We met in the dance. I was dancing. I was with somebody over there. And he was standing at the door and-- with a friend, and he say, come, we're going home. And he said, no, I want to meet her. She's-- Rose Ameisen here, I want to met her. And he stayed. And we met like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you spend a lot of time together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nearly we see each  other nearly every day later on, after a while. After a while, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the war three years into it, yes. We supposed to go Israel. But the war started, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that affect your relationship, the start of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war started, he used to come to us. My brother went to Russia, three of them. On motorbike they go. I send them, three of them. And I stayed myself at home. And he was coming. I say, listen. People know me here. You can't come like this to my place. Because for so many years ago-- we should get married, I told him. And we get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war started, '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Jewish wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it to me a little?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now Jewish. We went to the two rabbi, I think so, yes. And there was a huppa, everything. With everything like supposed to be. And I went later to have the other-- the other-- first was this wedding, I don't remember which wedding. And they asked me, are you really want to get married? Because I got my [NON-ENGLISH], yes. And they-- they don't-- I met our solicitor passing, and he come. Because I haven't got anybody. I have aunties, but they don't want me to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I didn't take them, you know? They say, they have me happy. They want have maybe somebody else or something. You know, always it's like this. Can you describe to me what was going on around you in terms of anti-Semitism? Did you experience any anti-Semitism in Krakow before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we live in the Polish, you know. Nothing [NON-ENGLISH] between-- but I never-- I was one Jewish girl in the class, you know? Even I have-- when they write the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the end of the year [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Report card?

speaker: interviewee
text: Report card, they write Christian. They didn't even remember that this was one Jewish girl in the class, you know. I-- I never-- I never had problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your friends know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well, always, always.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you hearing about Jews in other neighborhoods, in other areas, or in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, they-- we had people started to come before the war, German people and from Czechoslovakia. From Czechoslovakia during the war, but before the war, from Germany. We know, but we didn't realize, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you send your brothers to Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the German, we have said, they didn't do nothing to the women, only to the men, you know? That they take the men for works, and this. I was afraid something happen to them. And I told them, you run away. And they went to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with them when they left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They wrote the letters to me, cards. And after, one-- one brother went-- come back to Poland, because he has a wife. Some people come back. They make this mistake. And the other two, they went-- one went to [NON-ENGLISH]. Anyway to Russia. You know, they send him to Tashkent. He was everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: One died in the army. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you ever think of leaving Krakow yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think that you might leave Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't take--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think to yourself that-- did you ever try to plan-- make any arrangements to leave Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war? Yes. We have-- we supposed to-- my husband started to learn profession that he can do in Israel, you know. And we-- we supposed to go to Israel, because my sister was in Israel. We think about this. A lot of people were thinking. But you know, before you go, take time always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a visa to go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't. We didn't. We haven't been as far, but we think about to go. And we think too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to change your plans?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't realize that it's so close to us, you know? We didn't realize.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans invaded Krakow in September--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --1939. Can you describe to me what happened on that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was standing outside. And suddenly, the plane, you know, they started bombarding. Not far from us was the [NON-ENGLISH], the army was standing. How do you call it? And they started to bombard this-- this place. It was not exactly-- but you can see from the distance. Later it was the war. We couldn't believe it, but it was. And my husband was in the army. They take him to army. And he come to me. And I say, go and bring some gas mask. Because he say that we'll be-- and he run to bring-- before he come, I went away, you know. We take-- we have horses and errand. We couldn't take a car, but you take horses. And we run away. He come, and I was-- I was not at home anymore. And it's-- we go. We-- we travel. I change the horses with the gypsies. The gypsies give me very bad horses, I give them the good one, but tired, you know. It was not so easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you-- where were you running to go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know. We just-- we know the Germans coming this way. But whenever you go, the Germans come. And we stop and was bombarding in the meantime.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first contact with German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first, I tell you something. When I go and I want to sleep in the hotel, when there was a little hotel, and I come in. And I say to-- to the lady to show me the room, she show me, I said, this-- this seems not so clean. The-- the sheets are-- oh, no. Only three officers were sleeping here a few days before. I was, thank you. I sleep outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you eventually return to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. German soldiers bring me. Young pilots, you know, bring us back. Because we lost everything, what we have, in the meantime. And they took us. They didn't know that I'm a-- I'm a Jew. And they were very charming young boys, lost to, you know, the war. They miss their home, too. And they bring me to Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find Krakow? Did it change in the time you were gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not yet. It was, you know, week, or two weeks, maybe, I don't remember, 10 days. 10 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was life under the German occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well we started to wear the armbands straight away. And from the beginning, it was not different, you know. You live in your place, your house, your flat. There's nothing different. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you still have the soda water factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to sell to anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had been still in the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the-- the Jewish community in Krakow? Was there community leaders? And what was their response to the occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They couldn't do nothing. What they can do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there leadership?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. But don't ask me. I don't remember the names at all. I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any actions under the German occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any actions of when-- when I-- when started the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, in Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We went to ghetto, this what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the one year of war, I think we went to ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people were to country. Some people were to ghetto, depends when you can go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe to me how they took you to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: How we went to the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't remember, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you know that this was coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we-- we realize. We heard about this, yes. We didn't take nearly nothing with us. Or maybe we go-- I don't know how we go, by car, or by-- I don't remember this really. I don't remember how I go over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Podgorze, Podgorze.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this far from your street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, quite far. I mean, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not-- Krakow was quite a big city. But it was-- you can walk easily. But we should take something transport, you know. They take something we took with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you bring with you to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we have two bags, that's it, I think. But I don't know how to come over there. Maybe we come by horse, because I have the horses in the-- in the factory, you know, maybe they took us over there. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you when you went to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your aunts and uncles, where were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went to ghettos, too, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was the family together in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not together. I'm-- we don't live together. But my auntie was over there. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where's-- my husband's family was, too, in the ghetto, yes. His mother, his grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe to me what was the mood in the ghetto? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We have been, I tell you, very lucky when I read now about which-- which ghetto. You know, the book-- what's his name, I don't remember the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was beautiful. I mean, it was small ghetto, but nobody was hungry. You know, everybody managed to do something. We can go to the city. You can drink something. You know, it was still alive, I think, to the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of food were you eating in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever it was. I mean, horse sausage. My husband wants sausage, and he realize later that this is from the horse. But it was still, was all right. Meat, we have everything. It was a bakery downstairs. I take two [NON-ENGLISH] over there. You know, bread you have. It was-- it was-- thank god we have everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was guarding the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], Jewish policemen. And some Germans, but I never saw them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated by the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. I didn't see them. I mean, they have been a few of them in the ghetto. But nobody do nothing to me. And that's it, very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people leaving the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they going?

speaker: interviewee
text: To work. I was going to work, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cleaning in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Luftwaffe commander. Luftwaffe.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the airport? At the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Offices, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe a-- a typical day of work at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We leave early.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time would you wake up in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, 7:00-- he was working, sometimes, night time, my husband. And he come from work, and I go to work. And we see each other only a few minutes. Yes. And I come back around 4:00, 5:00. I cook something there, and he come. Now, he was coming in the morning. He left for work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find the work difficult?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. By matter of fact, we didn't work hard over there, you know. We're talking. Do-- we clean a few rooms. It was not much work. I was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with Polish citizens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice. I have very good friends. They bring me food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They come not in the ghetto. They come to the work where I was working, you know. And it was a German man over there, looking-- guarding us. But it was nothing. I can still talk with somebody who come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any newspapers or radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you listening to on the news? What were you hearing about the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait, we have radio? I don't remember that was allowed. Because once radio-- we have it in-- not in ghetto. And the German one day come knocked to the door and pick up our wireless. But in ghetto, I don't remember. Maybe it was not-- we are not allowed to have radio. I don't-- I don't-- I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have news of what was going on in the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely. Everybody, the people listen, you know? It was not allowed. But that people listen still to the radio. Everything we know was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what news were you hearing? What were you aware of?

speaker: interviewee
text: What you hear, that everything is-- that the German are going. And they take everything, whatever they can. Everything was good for them. Even when it was not good, they say it's good, you know, what you can hear. Never you see the truth in-- in wireless.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned there was Jewish police. Was there other-- were there other Jewish organizations in the ghetto? Was there a synagogue or school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Synagogue? You know, I don't think so, there was synagogue there. I didn't go over there. Maybe it was. Maybe people pray. You know, you don't need the synagogue to pray. 10 people, they can pray. But I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear of anybody escaping the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you can-- yes. And when to the ghetto, was people can go in and out, you know. Was very good German on the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: The guard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Guard. And people do what whatever they like. It was not so bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think of escaping the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Friend of mine, she run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: From work, from [NON-ENGLISH], she went to work. And one day, she run-- she-- they go here, she went this way, and vanished. It was a little bit problems. But she-- she went away, and she saved herself. She live in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luciana Wac.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wac.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think of escaping from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think of leaving this way?

speaker: interviewee
text: We still think. We always think about this, but never happened. We never have a carriage to go. People want to hide us here and take us there. But I don't trust them. I don't know. I was afraid. Maybe I was a coward. But when I go, I was thinking, what will be with my husband? You know, a friend of mine, she wants to run away. And she wants to take me with-- with her, and I told him. And he said, you go. You-- you save yourself. You do what you can do. But it was not so easy. I didn't want to go with her. She's alive, too. She live in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tosha, Tosha Bakschlak. It was her maiden name. Now, she-- I don't know what's her name. I used to know. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a hospital or a doctor in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was a hospital, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever need to visit the doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Thanks God, not. The hospital was not so easy. Not so-- it was danger to go to hospital, you know? German come, and they don't like sick people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: They can shot you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a German doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's a Jewish doctor. Even Polish doctor used to come to operate or something. They jump in and out. Yes. Professor Blaztel, he come to operate people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you never went--

speaker: interviewer
text: Very lovely.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never went to see the doctor in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where was your husband working at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: In some-- you know, in-- everything was car repair or something. Like some-- I don't remember where he was going, but he goes. I don't remember where-- where it was, exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he working for?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the-- for Wehrmacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: And OK, we'll stop here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.
